MARIN COUNTY
'9I 
REDWOOD HIGHWAY, 
'4OOO
MILL VALLEY, CAL]FORNIA 9{9'1
TELEPHONE (415) 3E9-6800
LAW OFFICES OF
NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO E MUELLER
A P iTNERSHIP INCLUDINC A PROTESSIONAL COAPORATION
770 L STREET, SUITE 600
6ACA,AMENTO, CALIIORNTA 9561'
TELEPHONE (976 ) 446 - 67 s2
IAN IBANCIsCO
650 CALIFOANIA STREET, SUTTE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CAIITORNIA 9'106
TELEPHONA (415) 989-6800
FPPC
OCT 1 0 tgsr
october 15, l-99L
l.tr. scott Hallabrin, Acting ceneral Counsel
Fair Po1itical Practices conmission
428 J Street, Suite 8OO
Sacranento, CA 98514
Re: Architects as Publ ic Officials ( ttconsultantstr )
Dear ur. Hallabrin:
We are requesting advice on behalf of a client which is an
architectural firn regarding the possible status of the firm,s
architects as public official,/rconsultantsrt under the Political
Reforn Act (Gov. Code 5 82048, 2 CCR S 18720(a) (2).) Based on
the Cornmission's Rupp Advice (No. 79-109), we have longr
understood that architects who work on specific projects for
public entities pursuant to a contract for architectural services
are not 'rconsultantsrt who are ttpublic officialstt under the Act.
We wish to confirm that understanding.
Our client is a corporation owned by two licensed
architects, each of rrhom is a 5Ot sharetrolder. Some of the
firm's architectural- services are provided to the State of
Californla pursuant to ttNe!, Schoo1 ProJ ectsrr agreements, wherein
a school district, as agent for the State Allocation Board,
contracts with an architect or architectural firn to design and
monitor the progress on the construction of a particular school ,
which is identified by nane in the contract. A copy of the
standard forn ttNew School Projectrt contract is attached and
incorporated by reference in this request. The form has been in
use since 1984 and is still 
used today.
The vast najority of school districts in the state do not
have tton stafftt architects who design and nonitor school
construction. Because the services of an architect are not
necessary unless the school distrlct is building a school,
architectural services of the type described in the attached
agreenent are customarily rendered pursuant to contracts with
outside firns. It is our understanding that since 1984,
thousands of the new schools constructed in California have been
funded by the State of California through the Leroy F. creene
State School Building L€ase Purchase Law of L976 referred to in
the attached agreenent. On all of these trNe!, School Projects,lr
architectural firms have signed agreements identical to the
attached. The standard Ne!, Schoo1 Project agreenent for


Mr. scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
October 15, 1991,
Page 2
architects was designed by the office of Local Assistance, a
subdivision of the state Allocation Board, under the aegis of the
Department of General Services, which is the ultirnate
adrninistrator of the New school Proj ect Lease-Purchase Program.
No variations in the terns of the agreeuent are pernitted.
Architectural firns shich enter into Neu school Proj ect
Agreenents are paid on a standard scheduLe which has been in use
for at least five years.
Although the trarchitecttt with rrhom the particular school
district contracts on behalf of the State in a New school Project
agreenent nay be a firn eroploying one or nore licensed
architects, state 1aw governing architectural services requires
that for each structure such as a new school, a single trarchitect
of recordrr sign design drawings and other docurnents required in
the perfornance of professional services. Thus, of the two
principals in the architectural firn lre represent, only one will
function as architect of record for a particular New school
Project. The seLection of the particular natural person within
the firn who will serve as ttarchitect of recordrt is nade by the
architectural finn.
It is our understanding that the Cornrpission has heretofore
not regarded an architect who renders only services such as
design and nonitoring to a public agency as a rrpublic
official/consultant, tr as those terns are used in Goverruoent Code
sections 82048, 87]-OO, 87302, and Title 2, sectj.on 18700 of the
California Code of Regulations. Our understanding is based on
past staff advice. For example, in 1979 the Connission advised
that lrhen an architectural firm contracted to nake design
proposals to a redevelopnent agency, the firn,s architects were
not public officials under the Act. The Connission staff
reasoned the architects were engaged to design a specific project
and $/ere not rendering or giving ongoing advice. Thus, the
architects could reconmend to the public agency a design
conpatible with that used by the developer rrho had enployed then
on an adjacent private project, even though the courpatible design
of public buildings could possibly enhance the val,ue of the
private nearby condoniniums owned by their private enployer.
(Rupp Advice, supra. A copy of this letter, and the request on
which it is based, is attached. )
The analysis in Rupp is sirnilar to that used in subsequent
FPPC advice letters to other professionals. For exanple, the
staff has advised that when a law firn is retained by a public
entity to draft a particular contract or handle a specific
lawsuit, the firn's attorneys are not consultants r^rho are public


Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel.
October L5, L99L
Paqe 3
officials under the Acti rather, as with many other vendors of
services, they have been engaged to deliver a rrproductrt -- that
is, representation to achieve the resolution of a particular
1ega1 dispute or natter. However, when a Iaw firn is retained to
give ongoing advice on a variety of subjects, the attorneys are
public officials, as they are serving as guasi-public agency
staff counsel . (Lara Advice, No. I-9o-277. ) Ilere, an
architectural fino is retained to provide standard architectural
services with respect to a Erlig}-aI 
New School -- not to advise
and consult generally. The architect under a New Schoo1
Agreement in effect contracts to deliver a product -- that is a
New SchooL, certified by the architect to be conplete. (see
Agreement, II D.4., final clause.)
Under a New School agreement, the client public agency
retains goverrunent powers to itself, the architect's function is
to provide independent, professional j udgnent. standard
architectural services on a lile!, School Proj ect include advice and
consultation with the client on utilization of the particular
site based on review of the needs to be met in the particular New
school , (Agreement, II A.1.); assistance in obtaining perroits and
approvals such as building and site access pernits, where
necessary (Agreement, II 8.3.); and input in preparing
specifications on bids for the prine building contract
(Agreement, II C.1.). Of course, the client must approve aL1 New
school plans, and the State alone, and not the architect, arrards
the actual building contract.
The services provided by an architectural firn on a Nerr
School Project are sinilar to those rendered by a law firrn in
litiqating a particular Datter, lrhere the firm may rnake
reconmendations to the client about the basis for a lawsuit, the
type of discovery to be conducted and the expense entailed, and
the advisability of accepting or rejecting a settlenent offer.
Holrever, lawyers in the firn offer their professionat judgment
about how to achieve the client's goaLs, and the client approves
only those suggestions with which it agrees. The architectural
firrn is sinilarly linited to design only within the parameters
set by its client. Like litigation attorneys, who are free to
choose which court reporting or other support service the law
firm will use, architects on a New School Project have heretofore
been free to sub-contract for support services based on their own
independent business and professionat j udgrnent, free of Politica1
Reforn Act conflict of interest concerns. (See Agreenent, III.)
We believe this is entirely appropriate. The public agency
client has detennined in advance the amount to be paid to the
architect for services, with the understanding that the architect


t[r. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
October 15, 1991
Page 4
will bear his own subcontracting expense on the particular New
School Proj ect.
Although the architect of a New school renders professional
services, the client deternines the scope of the project and
reviews and approves the architect's reconmendations.
The ctient under a New school ProJect has lts own
independent ttproject inspectortt (Agreement II, D.4.) and ls
ultinately responsible for decisions such as changes in design or
materials whlch may result in an increase in the overall costs of
the New school Project. (Agreenent II, D.4. [ trproposed change
orders for written approval of the clientrrl. An example of a
proposed trchange ordertr is attached) . Even if a rrchange orderrl
proposed by the architect results in no change in the cost of the
project or the amount of the construction contract, the change
order nust be approved by the Change order section of the state
office of Local Assistance. The client's independent lnspector
also signs rtcertificates of paynentr'r certifying as to the stages
of the Neu school Proj ect which have been conpleted, whlch
ttcertif icatesrt the architect prepares nonthly as part of his or
her basic architectural services. The rrcertLficates of paynentrr
are the basis on which nonies from the state are released to the
New school contractor under the terns of the separate standard
agreenent between the state Allocation Board (with the School
District as agent) and that contractor. However, the
ttcertificates of palmentrr do not change the overall amount due
the contractor from the state i they deternine only the tining of
the paynents to the contractor.
Although a New school Project is nulti-phasic, it is
durational. The relationship between the architect and the state
and school District agents terninates one year after issuance of
the architect's certificate of conpletion. (Agreenent II, D.5.)
The conmission has advised that even though a specific proj ect
nay be lengthy and may entail frequent consultation, when the
project involves professional services which are not merely an
extension of public agency staff services, the professionals
retained by the agency are not public official/consultants.
(Torres Advice, A-85-245).
Based on past advice, such as the Bgpp and other letters, it
would appear that the rarchitect of recordtl in an architectural
firn retained by a public agency to render professional services
for a Nelr school Project, and to provide architectural services
only in conjunction uith that Project, is not a rrpublic
official.tr 
However, we have been asked to request confirnation


l,!r. scott Hallabrin, Acting General counsel
October 15, 199L
Page 5
of that general principle. Therefore, we would appreciate your
response to the following questions:
ouestions
1. Is the trarchitect of recordt' whose firn has entered into
a standard [New Schoo1 Projectrr agreement (as attached) a
rrconsultantri who is a rtpublic officialrt within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act, and thus subj ect to economic interest
disclosure and disgual i fication?
2. ff the trarchitect of recordl ls a trconsultantrr who is a
ripublj.c official,rr which public agency designates the rrarchitect
of recordrt as a riconsultanttr under its conflict of interest code,
or anends its existing code to cover architects on lilew school
Projects? The Departnent of ceneral Services? The State
Allocation Board? Or the School District which acts as the
State's agent with respect to the particular Project?
We rrould appreciate a response to our inquiry at your
earliest convenience. We understand that Deanne Stone is the
attorney on your staff urost faniliar with these issues. Please
do not hesitate to caII should you, Ms. Stone or any other rnenber
of your staff need additional infornation regarding the
professional responsibilities of architects vis a vis New Schoo1
Proj ects .
YOU,
udith Sproul Davis
JSD/kab
6641.01
